# Release Notes

This page lists user-visible changes in each released version of SQL Lens. For the full developer-facing changelog, see `CHANGELOG.md` in the repository.

## May 22, 2026

- **[Fix] Interactive results now fit their frame** — On assistants that render inline app widgets (currently Claude Desktop and claude.ai), small `query_database` results, such as a two- or three-row table or a small chart, previously appeared with empty space around them or an extra scrollbar because the widget did not report its true height to the assistant. The widget now reports its own content size, so on hosts that support this a small result fits its frame with no surrounding empty space and no extra scrollbar, while larger results get the full height they need. No configuration change is required, and standalone viewers such as the MCP Inspector are unaffected. (#175)
- **[Improvement] The agent now looks up the schema instead of guessing column names** — When SQL Lens queries an unfamiliar database it has not seen before, it can now confirm a column or table name privately before retrying, for example after a query fails with an "Unknown column" error. Previously the agent was prevented from looking up the schema at all, so it would guess names and retry repeatedly until it ran out of steps. Those lookups stay internal and are never shown to you, and SQL Lens still declines requests to list tables, dump column names, or describe your schema. No configuration change is required. (#173)

## May 20, 2026

- **[Improvement] Executed SQL is now hidden from answers by default** — The `agent.show_details` setting now defaults to `false`, so a `query_database` answer no longer includes the SQL the agent ran. Exposing that SQL to connected clients can reveal details about your database structure and query logic, so it is now off unless you opt in. To restore the previous behavior and surface the executed SQL alongside each answer, set `show_details = true` in `sqllens.toml` or `SQLLENS_AGENT__SHOW_DETAILS=1`. **Action required if you relied on seeing the executed SQL:** set `show_details = true` after upgrading; otherwise answers are returned without the SQL block. (#152)
- **[Improvement] Saving question-and-answer patterns to memory is now opt-in** — A new `memory.save_queries` setting (default `false`) controls whether SQL Lens saves successful question-and-answer patterns to its memory. When off, no patterns are written automatically; SQL Lens still reads any patterns already in memory and still saves free-form notes, and you can seed patterns from a file with `sqllens import-memory` at any time. Set `save_queries = true` (or `SQLLENS_MEMORY__SAVE_QUERIES=1`) to let SQL Lens learn from successful queries as you go. See [Managing memory](managing-memory.md). (#152)
- **[Improvement] The assistant now keeps your database structure confidential** — SQL Lens instructs the agent not to expose your schema: it declines requests to list tables, dump column names, or describe how tables relate, and it will not run schema-listing queries on a user's behalf. This is a best-effort guardrail; to enforce exactly what is reachable, connect SQL Lens with a database user whose grants are limited to the specific tables or views you want queried. (#152)
- **[Fix] Chart generation with `visualize_data` no longer fails** — Asking SQL Lens for a chart could fail with an internal error instead of returning the chart, because the agent's attempt to remember the successful chart request was rejected during validation. The save step now accepts a request that carries no extra arguments, so `visualize_data` produces the chart and its summary as expected. No configuration change is required. (#147)
- **[Improvement] Charts and tables are now unified into the `query_database` tool** — The separate `visualize_data` tool has been folded into `query_database`, so SQL Lens now exposes two tools: `query_database` and `list_data_sources`. You no longer ask for a chart with a different tool — every `query_database` answer adapts to its result. On assistants that support inline app widgets (currently Claude Desktop and claude.ai), a single widget renders an interactive chart when the result is chart-shaped (aggregated or temporal) and an interactive, sortable table otherwise, with the same plain-text answer alongside. The assistant decides whether a chart fits; plain lookups are returned as a table or text. Supported chart types are unchanged (bar, line, area, scatter, pie, and heatmap, rendered with Apache ECharts), and no configuration change is required. (#150)
- **[Feature] Multi-turn conversations through the `query_database` tool** — The `query_database` tool now supports conversations that span several turns. When a question is ambiguous, the agent can ask a clarifying question (along with any suggested choices) and your assistant receives it as plain text, instead of the previous empty answer. Each answer also carries a conversation ID, returned both as structured data and as a short note at the end of the text answer; your assistant passes it back on the next question so the agent remembers the earlier turn, for example to answer its own clarifying question. Conversations are kept in memory only, cleared on restart, and capped by the new `agent.max_conversations` setting (default 1000, environment variable `SQLLENS_AGENT__MAX_CONVERSATIONS`). See [Multi-turn conversations](getting-started.md#multi-turn-conversations). (#151)

## May 19, 2026

- **[Feature] Row-Level Security for the SQL agent** — SQL Lens can now narrow every answer to the rows a particular request is allowed to see. Declare one or more `[[rls]]` blocks in `sqllens.toml` naming a `table`, `column`, `operator`, and either a static `value` or a per-request `value_from_metadata` key resolved from MCP `_meta`, and SQL Lens injects the predicate into every reference to the protected table — including inside subqueries, common table expressions, and joins — combined with whatever filter the assistant already produced using `AND`. Queries that cannot be safely scoped are blocked rather than run unfiltered. Static rules work on every transport; dynamic rules require the HTTP transport, where your embedding application asserts the requesting identity through `_meta`. The feature is opt-in: with no `[[rls]]` blocks, behavior is unchanged. See [Row-Level Security](row-level-security.md). (#136)
- **[Feature] `visualize_data` tool with interactive ECharts chart widget** — SQL Lens now exposes a third MCP tool, `visualize_data(question)`, that translates a chart-shaped natural-language question into SQL, executes it, and emits an interactive chart on assistants that support inline app widgets (currently Claude Desktop and claude.ai). Supported chart types are bar, line, area, scatter, pie, and heatmap, rendered with Apache ECharts; the widget follows the assistant's light or dark theme and resizes responsively. Every other assistant continues to receive the plain-text answer, and no configuration is required on your side — the tool is always registered alongside `query_database` and `list_data_sources`. The assistant calls `visualize_data` only when the result is aggregated or temporal and obviously chartable; plain lookups are answered as text. (#140)
- **[Feature] Executed SQL is now shown alongside `query_database` answers** — Every `query_database` answer now also surfaces the SQL that was executed to produce it, so you can spot-check the agent's interpretation before trusting the result. On any assistant the SQL appears as a fenced ```sql block under "Executed SQL:" in the answer; on assistants that render inline app widgets (Claude Desktop and claude.ai) it also appears as a collapsible "Executed SQL" section above the result grid. The new `agent.show_details` setting controls this (default `true`); set it to `false` in `sqllens.toml` or via `SQLLENS_AGENT__SHOW_DETAILS=0` to restore the previous answer-only output. Questions the agent answers without running SQL produce no block, with or without the setting. **Action required if you set `agent.show_sql`:** this setting replaces the previously reserved-but-unused `agent.show_sql` key from the May 18 release. Rename `show_sql` to `show_details` in your `sqllens.toml` (and any `SQLLENS_AGENT__SHOW_SQL` environment variable to `SQLLENS_AGENT__SHOW_DETAILS`); an unrenamed `show_sql` key is silently ignored, so the new default-on behavior will be in effect regardless. (#137)
- **[Feature] Interactive results widget for `query_database`** — On assistants that support inline app widgets — currently Claude Desktop and claude.ai — a `query_database` answer is now also displayed as an interactive table you can sort by column, filter with a search box, page through, and export to CSV, alongside the same plain-text answer. Every other assistant continues to receive the identical Markdown table as before, and no configuration or change is required on your side. This first release covers tables only; the new `visualize_data` tool adds chart widgets in #140. (#121)
- **[Improvement] Configuration errors fail closed so unrecognized error types cannot leak secrets** — Secret redaction in configuration error output now covers all configuration-load failures, not just field-validation errors. SQL Lens prints the error message only when its kind is known to be safe (such as a file-not-found, byte-order-mark, or syntax error); for any unrecognized error type that might quote a secret-bearing line from your file or environment, the message is withheld and a generic notice naming the fields to check (`api_key`, `bearer_token`, `database.url`) is shown instead. The same protection now also applies when `sqllens claude-desktop install` validates the configuration it generates, so an installer-time error cannot echo the API key you passed in. (#126)
- **[Feature] Readiness endpoint, host-header validation, and a plain-HTTP credential warning for the HTTP transport** — SQL Lens now serves an unauthenticated readiness endpoint at `GET /readyz` over the HTTP transport: it returns HTTP 503 with `{"status":"not ready"}` while the server is still warming up at startup (including a one-time embedding-model download of roughly 80 MB), then HTTP 200 with `{"status":"ready"}` once it is ready to serve queries, so Kubernetes readiness probes and load balancers can hold traffic back until startup finishes. The transport now also validates the request `Host` header against the configured host and loopback names, rejecting other hosts with HTTP 400 as a defense against DNS-rebinding (binding all interfaces with `0.0.0.0` accepts any host, as before); set `server.host` to your reverse-proxy domain when fronting SQL Lens. Finally, if bearer authentication is enabled while binding a non-loopback host, SQL Lens now logs a startup warning that credentials would travel in cleartext unless TLS is terminated in front of it. Like `/healthz`, `/readyz` requires no `Authorization` header and answers regardless of `Host`. (#113)
- **[Fix] Non-ASCII bearer tokens are now accepted** — When using bearer authentication over the HTTP transport, a token containing non-ASCII characters encoded as UTF-8 is now decoded correctly instead of being garbled, so it round-trips and matches. Existing ASCII tokens are unaffected. (#113)
- **[Feature] Memory import and export** — You can now seed SQL Lens with curated knowledge and back up what it has learned. The new `sqllens import-memory` and `sqllens export-memory` commands move question-and-answer pairs and free-form schema notes to and from a portable bundle file in JSON (lossless) or CSV (`question,sql` pairs only) format, with options for a dry run, clearing the store first, and batching large imports. Duplicate entries are skipped automatically. A new `[memory].allow_import` setting (default off) optionally exposes an `import_memory` tool to the connected assistant; leave it off unless every client is trusted, since a client that can write memory can influence future SQL generation. See [Managing memory](managing-memory.md). (#123)
- **[Improvement] First question over HTTP now answers quickly** — When SQL Lens runs over the HTTP transport, it now connects to your database, opens its memory store, and downloads the roughly 80 MB embedding model while the server is starting up, instead of on the first question your assistant asks. Previously that one-time cost of 30 to 60 seconds landed on the first `query_database` call; it is now paid during `sqllens serve` start-up so the first answer is fast. The warm-up is best-effort: if it cannot finish (for example, the database is briefly unreachable) the server still starts and the first question simply pays the cost and retries, exactly as before. Users running in stdio mode are unaffected. (#117)

## May 18, 2026

- **[Improvement] New optional configuration keys (all backward-compatible, no behavior change yet)** — SQL Lens accepts several new optional settings that reserve surface for upcoming features. A top-level `config_version` (defaults to `1`) lets future releases recognize the schema version of your `sqllens.toml`; it is accepted and currently ignored. `[server]` gains `log_level` (one of `critical`, `error`, `warning`, `info`, `debug`, `trace`; defaults to `info`). `[agent]` gains `show_sql` (defaults to `true`) and a new `[agent.audit]` section (`enabled` defaulting to `false`, plus `log_level`, `include_response_text`, and `sanitize_parameters`). Every key has a safe default, so existing configuration files and deployments continue to work unchanged and behave exactly as before — these settings do not yet change runtime behavior; the features that consume them ship in later releases. (#109)
- **[Fix] HTTP transport handles repeated lifespan events cleanly under unusual ASGI hosts** — When an ASGI host drives more than one lifespan scope against the same SQL Lens app (uncommon outside test harnesses), the HTTP transport now rejects a startup that arrives after the server has already shut down with the clear message `single-shot instance already shut down`, instead of the misleading `duplicate lifespan.startup` it returned previously. A repeated shutdown is acknowledged as `lifespan.shutdown.complete` without re-entering the session manager's exit handler, and startup failures now report the exception type in addition to the message (for example, `RuntimeError: boom`). Users running `sqllens serve` are not affected — uvicorn drives exactly one lifespan per process. (#70)
- **[Feature] Query timeouts and row caps in every SQL runner** — SQL Lens now enforces a per-query statement timeout (default 30 seconds) and a hard ceiling on rows returned (default 10000) on Postgres, MySQL, and SQLite. When a result is trimmed, the assistant is told the answer was truncated so it can re-issue a narrower query. Tune the new `database.statement_timeout_ms` and `database.max_rows` fields in `sqllens.toml`, or override per deployment with `SQLLENS_DATABASE__STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_MS` and `SQLLENS_DATABASE__MAX_ROWS`. (#45)
- **[Fix] Postgres and MySQL query errors are no longer masked by cleanup-time exceptions** — When a query against Postgres or MySQL failed mid-execution (for example, a statement timeout, a dropped connection, or an aborted transaction), a secondary error raised while closing the cursor or connection could replace the original error before it reached your assistant. The assistant then saw a generic transport error such as "broken pipe on close" instead of the actual cause and could not re-plan with a tighter `WHERE` or `LIMIT`. The original query error now reaches the assistant intact, and cleanup-time failures are recorded in the server log as warnings so chronic teardown problems remain diagnosable. The Postgres connector also no longer leaks a database connection when allocating the cursor itself fails. (#61)
- **[Fix] Memory feature: text-memory tool now wired up and `similarity_threshold` setting now honored** — SQL Lens can now save free-form text notes (for example, "in this schema, `cust_seg` means customer segment") so the assistant can lean on domain vocabulary across future questions. Previously the tool that saves these notes existed in the code but was never registered, so the assistant never used it. In addition, the `[memory].similarity_threshold` setting (and `SQLLENS_MEMORY__SIMILARITY_THRESHOLD`) now actually controls the default similarity floor used when SQL Lens searches its memory; before this fix the configured value was ignored at runtime and a hardcoded `0.7` was used instead. (#77)
- **[Fix] `auth.mode = "jwt"` is now rejected at configuration time instead of starting a server that rejects every request** — JWT authentication is reserved but not yet implemented. Previously a configuration with `auth.mode = "jwt"` passed `sqllens validate` with `Config OK`, the server started, and every request was rejected with a 401 at runtime. Both `sqllens validate` and `sqllens serve` now fail immediately with the message `auth.mode="jwt" is not implemented yet; use "bearer" or "none"`, so the misconfiguration surfaces before deployment instead of after. (#97)
- **[Improvement] Bearer tokens must be at least 16 characters** — When `auth.mode = "bearer"`, SQL Lens now rejects a token shorter than 16 characters (measured after surrounding whitespace is trimmed), in addition to the existing checks for a missing or blank token. A short shared secret is trivially brute-forceable, so the error recommends generating a strong random token with `openssl rand -hex 32`, and the configuration file written by `sqllens init` now includes that guidance. (#97)
- **[Improvement] Configuration validation errors no longer echo secret values** — When a field fails schema validation, `sqllens validate` and `sqllens serve` now report only the field location, the reason, and the error type. The rejected value is no longer printed, so a bearer token, Anthropic API key, or a password embedded in a database connection URL is not exposed in error output or logs. (#97)
- **[Improvement] `sqllens validate` now uses three exit codes** — `sqllens validate` exits `0` when the configuration is valid and the server would start, `1` when the configuration parses correctly but the server would refuse to start because `llm.api_key` is not set (the `Config OK` summary still prints, followed by a `Would fail to start:` notice), and `2` when the file fails to parse or a field is invalid. Continuous integration pipelines can now distinguish a structurally broken configuration from one that is fine but not yet wired with a key. `sqllens serve` exit codes are unchanged. (#97)
- **[Fix] HTTP transport handles interrupted startup cleanly when mounted under an external ASGI host** — If a host that mounts the SQL Lens ASGI app cancels lifespan startup mid-way (for example, a startup timeout or a shutdown signal arriving while the server is still booting), the transport now lets that cancellation propagate cooperatively instead of reporting startup as completed. The interrupted instance is finalized so a later startup is cleanly rejected and a later shutdown is a no-op, avoiding a session manager left in an unknown state. Users running `sqllens serve` are not affected — uvicorn drives exactly one lifespan per process. (#100)
- **[Fix] Query errors no longer leak database connection details, and failure types are now distinguishable** — When a question could not be answered, SQL Lens previously returned the raw underlying exception to your assistant, which for database driver failures could include the host, port, database name, and role from the connection string. Infrastructure and internal failures now return a stable `internal error; see server logs` message instead, with the full detail recorded only in the server log. Genuine SQL execution problems reported by the agent are still passed through (prefixed with `SQL execution error:`) and read-only safety rejections are still surfaced word for word, so your assistant can tell an environment problem apart from a query it should rewrite. Under heavy concurrent load, the first several questions also no longer each try to build the agent independently. (#96)
- **[Fix] HTTP transport reports shutdown without prior startup instead of masking it** — When an ASGI host drives a `lifespan.shutdown` event without ever sending a `lifespan.startup` first (a sign the host is misbehaving), the HTTP transport now replies `lifespan.shutdown.failed` with the message `shutdown without prior startup` and logs a warning, instead of silently acknowledging `lifespan.shutdown.complete` as it did before. This makes a faulty embedding host visible rather than hiding the bug behind a clean acknowledgment. Users running `sqllens serve` are not affected — uvicorn drives exactly one lifespan per process. (#87)
- **[Feature] Container health check endpoint** — SQL Lens now serves an unauthenticated liveness endpoint at `GET /healthz` over the HTTP transport, returning HTTP 200 with the body `{"status":"ok"}` while the server is running. No `Authorization` header is required even when bearer authentication is enabled, so Docker, Kubernetes, and load balancer health probes work without a token. The published Docker image's built-in health check now uses this endpoint and no longer suppresses failures, so a container that has stopped serving is correctly reported as unhealthy to your orchestrator. (#95)
- **[Security] Read-only guard hardened against side-effecting functions, plus a connector-level read-only backstop** — The read-only SQL guard previously inspected only the statement shape, so a syntactically valid `SELECT` that *called* a dangerous function slipped through. SQL Lens now also rejects, at parse time and per database dialect, known side-effecting / denial-of-service / remote-code-execution functions — including SQLite `load_extension` (arbitrary code execution on the default SQLite deployment), Postgres `pg_read_file`, `pg_sleep`, `dblink_exec`, `lo_import`/`lo_export` and friends, MySQL `sleep`, `load_file`, `benchmark`, and `generate_series` on any dialect. When the dialect is unknown the union of every denylist is applied (fail-closed). In addition, when `database.read_only` is enabled, read-only is now enforced a second time at the database connection itself — SQLite opens read-only and sets `PRAGMA query_only`, Postgres uses a read-only session, MySQL uses a read-only transaction — so a write can no longer reach your database even if it somehow bypasses the parser guard. The guard also now fails closed (refuses the query) on any unexpected internal error instead of crashing. No configuration change is required; read-only deployments are strictly safer. (#103)

## May 17, 2026

- **[Fix] Read-only guard now rejects `SELECT ... INTO` on Postgres and T-SQL** — The read-only SQL guard previously accepted statements like `SELECT * INTO new_table FROM users`, which create a new table on Postgres and T-SQL (including `INTO TEMP` and `INTO UNLOGGED` variants) and write a session variable on MySQL (`SELECT ... INTO @var`). These statements are now rejected at parse time, including when nested inside common table expressions or used as an operand of `UNION`, `INTERSECT`, or `EXCEPT`. This restores the documented "read-only by default" guarantee for the default configuration. (#41)

## May 16, 2026

- **[Feature] One-command Claude Desktop setup** — Connecting SQL Lens to Claude Desktop is now a single command: `sqllens claude-desktop install --db <url>`. The installer writes a configuration file, merges an entry into Claude Desktop's settings while preserving any existing preferences and other MCP servers, and saves a timestamped backup of the settings file before changing it. On Windows, the installer also generates the launcher script that was previously assembled by hand. Run with `--dry-run` to preview the changes before they are written. Works on Windows, macOS, and Linux. (#25)
- **[Fix] Tool errors now surface verbatim instead of being paraphrased** — When a tool call fails, SQL Lens now quotes the underlying error message in a fenced code block and asks how you want to proceed, instead of inventing a plausible-sounding root cause. This makes failures, such as access-denied errors on per-query scratch files, directly debuggable rather than misleading. (#20)

## Unreleased

- **[Fix] `build_asgi_app` now works when mounted under FastAPI or another ASGI host** — Mounting the SQL Lens ASGI app inside an external host previously failed on the first request with an opaque message from the MCP SDK (`Task group is not initialized. Make sure to use run().`) because the session-manager lifespan wrapper was applied only inside `sqllens serve`. The wrapper is now part of `build_asgi_app` itself, so the returned app is mount-ready under uvicorn, FastAPI, Starlette, and other ASGI hosts. Users running `sqllens serve` are not affected. (#43)
- **[Improvement] Eager preflight for database, LLM, memory, and auth on `sqllens serve`** — `sqllens serve` now checks that the database is reachable, the Anthropic client constructs cleanly, the Chroma memory directory is writable, and the configured authenticator is valid before binding the transport. Misconfiguration now exits with code 2 and a `Preflight failed: <subsystem>: <detail>` message at startup, instead of surfacing as an opaque error on the first question your assistant asks. Pass `--no-preflight` or set `SQLLENS_NO_PREFLIGHT=1` to skip the checks. `sqllens validate` gains `--check-db`, `--check-llm`, `--check-memory`, and `--check-auth` flags so the same checks can run in CI without starting the server. (#44)
- **[Fix] Security: refuse to start an unauthenticated HTTP server on a non-loopback host** — `sqllens serve` now exits with a clear error when `server.transport = "http"`, `auth.mode = "none"`, and `server.host` is not a loopback address. This prevents the most common accidental exposure path — a container that binds `0.0.0.0` so the port can be published — from publishing an unauthenticated SQL endpoint. The recommended fix is to set `SQLLENS_AUTH__MODE=bearer` together with a random `SQLLENS_AUTH__BEARER_TOKEN`; closed-network deployments can set `SQLLENS_AUTH__INSECURE=1` (or `auth.insecure = true` in `sqllens.toml`) to acknowledge the choice, in which case SQL Lens prints a warning at startup but continues. The README Docker quick-start has been updated to demonstrate the bearer-token pattern. (#48)
- **[Improvement] `sqllens validate` surfaces the loopback-guard policy** — `sqllens validate` now applies the same loopback safety check as `sqllens serve`: a configuration with `transport = "http"`, `auth.mode = "none"`, and a non-loopback `server.host` fails validation with exit code 2 instead of passing silently. Continuous integration pipelines and pre-deployment lint steps can now catch the misconfiguration before `sqllens serve` would refuse to start. Closed-network deployments can acknowledge the configuration by setting `SQLLENS_AUTH__INSECURE=1` (or `auth.insecure = true` in TOML); validation then succeeds and prints a visible breadcrumb on the `auth:` summary line. (#52)
- **[Feature] `--version` flag on the sqllens CLI** — Running `sqllens --version` now prints the installed version and exits, matching common CLI conventions. The existing `sqllens version` subcommand is preserved. (#19)
- **[Improvement] Ship stdio Claude Desktop example and add Windows config path to README** — A new `examples/mcp-clients/claude_desktop_stdio.json` snippet steers Claude Desktop users onto the recommended stdio launch pattern (simpler than HTTP — no port management), and the existing HTTP example is renamed to `claude_desktop_http.json` to make the two variants explicit. The README and getting-started guide now list the Windows config path `%APPDATA%\Claude\claude_desktop_config.json` alongside the macOS path, and point Windows users at the dedicated install guide that documents the `.cmd` launcher workaround for the non-writable working-directory issue. (#22)
- **[Fix] Access-denied errors on every query under Claude Desktop on Windows** — SQL Lens previously wrote per-query scratch CSVs into its current working directory, which under Claude Desktop on Windows is the launcher's install folder and is not writable by the user. Every query failed with `[WinError 5] Access is denied`. Scratch files are now written into your user temp directory regardless of how the server is launched, so Claude Desktop on Windows works out of the box without the `.cmd` wrapper workaround. (#21)
- **[Improvement] Better config error messages: detect UTF-8 BOM in TOML, make `llm.api_key` optional for `validate`** — `sqllens validate` no longer requires `llm.api_key`, so you can lint configuration files without exporting a secret first. `sqllens serve` still requires the key and now points you at both the `SQLLENS_LLM__API_KEY` environment variable and the `[llm].api_key` TOML key when it is missing. When `sqllens.toml` starts with a UTF-8 byte-order mark, both commands now name the BOM by name and print rewrite commands for PowerShell 7+, PowerShell 5.1, and bash, replacing the previous opaque "Invalid statement (at line 1, column 1)" error. (#24)
- **[Fix] Bearer mode no longer starts silently with a missing or blank token** — Previously, setting `auth.mode = "bearer"` without a usable `bearer_token` (missing, empty, or whitespace-only) would let SQL Lens start cleanly and then reject every incoming request at runtime with no startup signal. Both `sqllens serve` and `sqllens validate` now refuse the configuration at load time with an actionable message that names the `SQLLENS_AUTH__BEARER_TOKEN` environment variable, the `[auth]` section of `sqllens.toml`, and the alternate `mode` values (`none` or `jwt`). (#51)
- **[Fix] Reject `auth.bearer_token` set without `auth.mode = "bearer"`** — Setting `auth.bearer_token` (or the `SQLLENS_AUTH__BEARER_TOKEN` environment variable) while `auth.mode` is `"none"` or `"jwt"` is now rejected at config load with an actionable error. Previously the token was silently ignored, so an operator who set only the environment variable expecting to enable bearer auth ended up running an unauthenticated server under the default `mode = "none"`. This is a breaking change for any deployment that was relying on the silent-ignore behavior: either set `auth.mode = "bearer"` to use the token, or remove `bearer_token` and unset `SQLLENS_AUTH__BEARER_TOKEN`. (#55)

## 0.0.2 — 2026-04-28

This release fixed two release-pipeline issues that affected the first published version.

- The Docker image for version 0.0.1 was not published to GHCR because the release workflow lacked the permission required to attach the software bill of materials. The 0.0.2 Docker image is available and signed.
- The MCPB bundle is now built from the current directory rather than a `file://` URL. This fixes an install failure on Windows builders where Python rejected the MSYS-style path.

## 0.0.1 — 2026-04-28

Initial public release.

### Features

- A natural-language SQL agent that translates questions to SQL, executes them, and returns results as a Markdown table.
- Two authentication modes for the HTTP transport: `none` and `bearer`. JWT support is scaffolded but not yet implemented.
- A read-only SQL guard that parses the generated query and rejects anything that is not a `SELECT`.
- A Streamable HTTP transport with authentication middleware and a path normalizer that accepts both `/mcp` and `/mcp/`.
- Drop-in MCP client configuration snippets for Cursor, Claude Desktop, Windsurf, and stdio launchers.

### Install paths

Three install paths are produced by the same release pipeline:

- **PyPI**: `pip install sqllens[all]` then `sqllens serve`.
- **Docker**: `docker run` against the published GHCR image. The image supports both `amd64` and `arm64` and is signed with cosign. A software bill of materials is attached to each release.
- **MCPB**: Drag the platform-specific bundle onto Claude Desktop for a one-click install. Builds are available for macOS (x86_64 and arm64), Linux (x86_64), and Windows (x86_64).

## See also

- **[Getting started](getting-started.md)** to install the latest release.
- **[Configuration reference](configuration.md)** for the current set of configuration fields.
